headline: Lamda wins cloud deal with Hudson River Trading to supply access to Nvidia chips
summary: Artificial intelligence cloud computing startup Lamda on Wednesday said ‌it has won a cloud contract with high-speed ‌trading firm Hudson River Trading.  Lamba, which is backed by Nvidia and ​raised $1.5 billion last year after signing a deal to provide Microsoft with access to Nvidia's chips, said the deal with Hudson River Trading will entail renting ‌more than 1,000 of ⁠Nvidia's latest "Blackwell" systems to the trading firm, which Reuters last month reported had $12.3 billion ⁠in trading revenues last year.  Lamda and Hudson River Trading did not disclose the financial terms of the ​deal.

content.title: Lamda wins cloud deal with Hudson River Trading to supply access to Nvidia chips
content.summary: Artificial intelligence cloud computing startup Lamda on Wednesday said ‌it has won a cloud contract with high-speed ‌trading firm Hudson River Trading. Lamda and Hudson River Trading did not disclose the financial terms of the ​deal.

headline: Thales inks deal with Google for homegrown German cloud provider
summary: French defence group Thales and Alphabet's Google Cloud have ‌signed a deal to launch a ‌new European cloud service in Germany that will be ​operationally and legally independent from Google, the companies said on Wednesday.  The European Union has been pushing to develop homegrown technology ‌solutions as it ⁠seeks to reduce reliance on foreign providers, particularly in cloud computing, ⁠a sector dominated by large U.S. companies.

content.title: Thales inks deal with Google for homegrown German cloud provider

The European Union has been pushing to develop homegrown technology ‌solutions as it ⁠seeks to reduce reliance on foreign providers, particularly in cloud computing, ⁠a sector dominated by large U.S. companies.

headline: Google courts coders and consumers at I/O, touts cheaper AI model
summary:
STORY: :: Google

Alphabet's Google on Tuesday put AI agents directly into its search box and rolled out a faster, cheaper version of its Gemini model.

That's as it looks to to blunt gains by rivals Anthropic and OpenAI among enterprise customers.

During its annual I/O developer conference in California, Google launched Gemini 3.5 Flash, a model designed for coding and automated tasks, and said 3.5 Pro was coming next month.

The tech giant also introduced Gemini Spark, a new agent that pulls information across its suite of products to draft reports and manage schedules, as well as new video model Gemini Omni.

The company also lowered the cost of its top-tier AI Ultra subscription plan to $200 a month from $250.

It also announced a $100-a-month version of the Ultra subscription it said was tailored for developers and other work-related users.

As competition shifts to big-spending business customers, Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai highlighted lower costs.

"We've heard that many companies are already blowing through their annual token budgets, and it's only May. If companies used a mix of flash and other frontier models they could save a lot of money. To put this in perspective, top companies in Google Cloud are processing about 1 trillion tokens a day. If they shifted 80% of their workloads from other frontier models to 3.5 Flash, they'd save over $1 billion annually."

Google also reinforced its core consumer products including YouTube and Search...

... touting new features including a set of agents that will autonomously complete tasks like making purchases, monitoring tickets, and planning schedules in real time.

Search was Alphabet’s biggest revenue driver in 2025, when it reported over $400 billion in total revenue.
